title: Association between denosumab use and risk of osteoarthritis among adults with osteoporosis in a real-world cohort
research_question: To estimate the effect of denosumab compared with bisphosphonates on reducing the risk of knee or hip osteoarthritis among adults with osteoporosis in a real-world cohort.
methods_summary: New-user, active-comparator retrospective real-world cohort study using electronic health records from the TriNetX Network.
Participants diagnosed with osteoporosis between January 1, 2014 and December 31, 2023 were selected.
Propensity-score matching (PSM) was used to balance potential confounders between denosumab users and bisphosphonate users.
Cox proportional hazards models were used to estimate hazard ratios (HRs) and 95% confidence intervals (CIs) of incident knee and hip OA.
dataset: TriNetX US Collaborative Network (electronic health records from 66 large healthcare organizations across the United States)
key_findings: Denosumab use was associated with a reduced risk of any OA (HR 0.96, 95% CI 0.94-0.98) and knee OA (HR 0.87, 95% CI 0.84-0.91) compared with bisphosphonates.
No significant association was found between denosumab use and hip OA (HR 0.98, 95% CI 0.94-1.03) or thumb carpometacarpal OA (HR 0.94, 95% CI 0.87-1.01).
The association was more pronounced among older participants (≥65 years), females, and individuals of Asian ethnicity.
extracted_insights: Denosumab, a RANKL inhibitor used for osteoporosis, may reduce the risk of incident knee OA compared to bisphosphonates, especially in older adults, females, and Asian individuals, suggesting a potential disease-modifying effect for knee OA that warrants further clinical trials.
